dominant_error_pattern: Misframes CBM's individual student-progress function as either school-wide standards auditing, teacher/student quality evaluation, or a complete replacement for standardized testing ecosystems.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A redefines CBM as a school accountability tool instead of individual skill tracking. Clarify: CBM documents *student* growth on *discrete skills*, not school-wide standards compliance. Distractor B hijacks CBM for teacher-evaluation purposes; prevent this misuse by emphasizing student response-to-instruction, not educator appraisal. Distractor D claims CBM fully supplants large-scale testing; note: CBM supplements decision-making, does not replace standardized systems.

dominant_error_pattern: Conflates CBM's core capability—continuous progress monitoring with dynamic goal adjustment—with norm-referenced benchmarking, proof of teaching quality, or curriculum validation.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B expects norm tables from a tool engineered for individual growth lines, not peer comparison. Teach: CBM shows *this student's* weekly rise, not national percentiles. Distractor C misinterprets slopes as teacher-quality data; emphasize: graphs document student response patterns, not educator skill. Distractor D asks CBM to prove curriculum reliability/validity; clarify: brief probes cannot certify curriculum psychometrics—different purpose entirely.

dominant_error_pattern: Narrows CBM's scope to reading-only RtI, redefines it as class-wide program evaluator, or mistakes CBA as exclusively special ed eligibility assessment instead of understanding CBA as the broad umbrella program encompassing CBM as one tool.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A limits CBM to reading interventions, ignoring identical measurement logic for math/spelling. Teach: CBM is domain-portable; same framework applies across academic areas. Distractor C treats CBM as a class-wide program-outcome measure; clarify: CBMs measure individual response, not aggregate program effect. Distractor D mislabels CBA as a special ed gate; reinforce: CBA is a broad assessment *process* that may include CBMs, classroom observations, and other tools.

dominant_error_pattern: Selects a DIBELS example with national norms instead of curriculum-based passages, chooses semester-length pre-post comparison instead of frequent short samples, or picks state-standards-referenced one-off testing instead of repeated curriculum measurement.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A cites DIBELS's national norming and standard scores; note: while DIBELS can be frequent, this example emphasizes national comparison, not curriculum-local repeated sampling. Prefer locally-administered, curriculum-sourced probes. Distractor B shows two semester tests with no intermediate probes; emphasize: CBM's advantage is weekly frequency, not endpoint-only contrasts. Distractor D uses state proficiency standards on a single classroom test; shift focus to repeated curriculum passages compared to class baseline, not one-time state cut-scores.
